story:
The sharp snap of his client’s laces cut through the stale room. Kye sat against the headboard, a sheet loose across his waist, his face arranged into calm. Underneath, humiliation burned. He kept his eyes averted as the man wrestled with buttons stretched over his belly, and the smile in his voice felt almost predatory.

“I’ll look for you again,” the man said, voice gravelly.

“Sure,” Kye replied, turning to the window, letting the promise hang like a threat.

Cash rustled onto the mattress. The door closed.

He stared at his shoes—scuffed, thinned by long hours. Every scrape was a record of survival. Kye did what he had to do. His father had left; someone had to keep the lights on. But responsibility wasn’t the whole of it. Inside was a quieter, sharper fear: becoming invisible. He wanted to matter. He thought of his mother’s tired smile, the way her strength held the house together. He wanted to be that anchor, to break the old pattern, to wrest control from a life that always tried to decide for him.

He showered. Cool water hissed against hot skin. Shame loosened and spiraled down the drain. He braced his forehead on his arm while rivulets carried his secrets to the bottom of Bellmore City. When he stepped back into the night—hood up, hands deep in his pockets—he passed through the grimy, hidden side of wealth. He kept his head down and moved toward the other world he knew, one that, at least for a while, felt clean.

His thoughts drifted to Billy. Billy, who didn’t judge. Who respected boundaries and listened. Who could make him laugh when laughter had been stolen. From the start, Billy felt different—solid, safe. Those feelings grew, slowly at first, then all at once. It was like trying to climb a glass wall and sliding back down, palms stinging. Watching Billy with other boyfriends—Dagger was the last—made him feel smaller, unworthy. The weight inside pressed against his ribs. Days felt shorter. The light thinned.

Kye swallowed his silence like thorns. He told himself it was a crush, a passing itch. But the ache stayed. “Blood-soaked petals” was how it felt—beauty and pain crushed together so he wouldn’t speak. Sometimes he borrowed courage from a bottle—barley-born bravado that blurred what was wise.

One night he rode to Billy’s, drank more, and slipped into the pool. The water steadied him. When Billy came out, Kye rose, heart hammering, and closed the space between them. He kissed him. Billy froze, startled—blue eyes wide, kind even then. Kye pulled back. The thought hit him: I don’t know how to fix this. Shame and longing collided. He hadn’t meant to cross a line, and he knew it. He would bury the moment like treasure on a far island—kept, but hidden. He would protect the friendship, pretend the night was a blur he barely remembered. The truth would haunt only him.

He reached his moped and looked down the alley. Neon buzzed and flickered over wet pavement. Other workers leaned into windows, laughed too brightly, bartered quiet pieces of themselves. He wondered what armor their secrets made. The first drops fell—here, then there—clouds rumbling as if hungry for everything the city hid. He turned the key. The engine sputtered like a small rebellion. As he pulled away, the rain began to wash the streets, and in the damp air a faint aftertaste lingered—something almost like hope.
